---
title: "Day 2 — Warm-up quiz: ownership and borrowing"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

A short multiple-snippet quiz to warm up your borrow-checker intuition before the day's hands-on exercises. Read each snippet, decide whether it compiles (and if not, why), then expand the answer.

15 questions, gentle to tricky. Don't skip — if you can answer them all, the afternoon's exercises will land much faster.

A few notes:

- Every answer expands inline; resist the temptation to peek before guessing.
- "Compiles" here means *with the current borrow checker (NLL — non-lexical lifetimes)*. Rust got smarter about borrow scopes a few years ago; the answers reflect modern Rust.
- A few questions ask about an expression's behaviour, not just whether it compiles.

---

## 1. Copying a small value

```rust
let x: u8 = 30;
let y = x;
println!("{} {}", x, y);
```

Does this compile? What does it print?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
Is `u8` a `Copy` (duplicated on assignment, not moved) type?
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**Yes, it compiles, and prints `30 30`.** `u8` implements [`Copy`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/marker/trait.Copy.html), so `let y = x;` duplicates the byte rather than moving ownership. Both `x` and `y` remain valid afterwards.
:::

---

## 2. Moving a `Vec`

```rust
let v = vec![1, 2, 3];
let w = v;
println!("{:?}", v);
```

Does this compile?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
`Vec<T>` does not implement `Copy` — assigning it moves ownership.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**No.** `Vec<u8>` owns a heap allocation (the bytes live on the heap; the stack header holds `ptr`, `len`, `cap`). `let w = v;` transfers that ownership to `w`; the binding `v` is now invalid and the compiler refuses to print it (`error[E0382]: borrow of moved value: \`v\``).
:::

---

## 3. Passing a `Vec` into a function

```rust
fn take(v: Vec<u8>) {
    println!("len = {}", v.len());
}

let v = vec![1, 2, 3];
take(v);
println!("{:?}", v);
```

Does this compile?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
What does the function signature say about ownership of `v`?
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**No.** The function `take` takes `v` by value (consumes it), so calling `take(v)` moves ownership into the function. After the call, `v` in the caller is gone and the final `println!` is a compile error.
:::

---

## 4. Passing a `Vec` by shared reference

```rust
fn read(v: &Vec<u8>) {
    println!("len = {}", v.len());
}

let v = vec![1, 2, 3];
read(&v);
println!("{:?}", v);
```

Does this compile?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
`&Vec<u8>` is a *shared borrow* — `read` looks at the Vec without taking ownership.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**Yes.** `read` borrows `v` shared-and-read-only for the duration of the call. After the call returns, the borrow ends and `v` is still owned by the caller, so the final `println!` is fine.
:::

---

## 5. Two shared borrows at once

```rust
let v = vec![1, 2, 3];
let a = &v;
let b = &v;
println!("{:?} {:?}", a, b);
```

Does this compile?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
The borrow rule is "any number of readers OR exactly one writer."
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**Yes.** Both `a` and `b` are shared (`&T`) borrows. You can have as many shared borrows as you like simultaneously; they're read-only views and can't conflict.
:::

---

## 6. Mixing shared and mutable borrows

```rust
let mut v = vec![1, 2, 3];
let a = &v;
let b = &mut v;
println!("{:?} {:?}", a, b);
```

Does this compile?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
"Any number of readers OR exactly one writer" — never both.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**No.** A `&mut v` cannot coexist with any other live borrow, shared or mutable. The compiler rejects this with `error[E0502]: cannot borrow \`v\` as mutable because it is also borrowed as immutable`.
:::

---

## 7. Two mutable borrows at once

```rust
let mut v = vec![1, 2, 3];
let a = &mut v;
let b = &mut v;
a.push(4);
b.push(5);
```

Does this compile?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
"Exactly one writer."
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**No.** Only one outstanding `&mut` borrow may exist at a time. Two `&mut`s to the same value is rejected (`error[E0499]: cannot borrow \`v\` as mutable more than once at a time`).
:::

---

## 8. Borrows end at their last use (NLL)

```rust
let mut v = vec![1, 2, 3];
let a = &v;
println!("{:?}", a);   // last use of `a`
let b = &mut v;
b.push(4);
```

Does this compile?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
Modern Rust ends a borrow at its *last use*, not at the end of the lexical scope. This is called NLL (non-lexical lifetimes — borrow lives only while you actually need it).
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**Yes.** Even though `a` is declared before `b` lexically, the compiler sees that `a`'s last use is the `println!`. The shared borrow ends there, so by the time `&mut v` is created, no other borrow is alive.
:::

---

## 9. Slicing a `Vec`

```rust
let v: Vec<u8> = vec![10, 20, 30, 40];
let s: &[u8] = &v[1..3];
println!("{:?}", s);
```

Does this compile? What does it print?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
`&v[1..3]` is a shared borrow of a sub-range of the Vec.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**Yes, it compiles, and prints `[20, 30]`.** `&v[1..3]` produces a `&[u8]` (a *fat pointer* — pointer plus length, 16 bytes on a 64-bit machine) that views elements at indices 1 and 2 (the end is exclusive).
:::

---

## 10. Method calls and implicit borrows

```rust
let mut v = vec![1, 2, 3];
v.push(4);
println!("{:?}", v);
```

What kind of borrow does `v.push(4)` take?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
Look at the signature: [`Vec::push(&mut self, value: T)`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/vec/struct.Vec.html#method.push).
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**Mutable.** `push` is declared `fn push(&mut self, value: T)`, so the method call `v.push(4)` is equivalent to `Vec::push(&mut v, 4)` — a mutable borrow of `v` for the duration of the call. The borrow ends as soon as `push` returns, so the next `println!` is fine.
:::

---

## 11. Moving out of a `Vec` by indexing

```rust
let v: Vec<String> = vec![String::from("A"), String::from("C")];
let s: String = v[0];
println!("{}", s);
```

Does this compile?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
Indexing a `Vec<String>` would *move* a `String` out, leaving a hole. Rust doesn't allow holes inside Vecs.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**No.** `v[0]` would move the `String` out of the Vec, but that would leave the Vec in an undefined state (a hole at index 0). The compiler rejects this with `error[E0507]: cannot move out of index of \`Vec<String>\``.

The fixes: borrow instead (`let s: &String = &v[0];`), clone (`let s = v[0].clone();`), or consume the whole Vec (`let s = v.into_iter().next().unwrap();`).
:::

---

## 12. Returning a reference to a local

```rust
fn make() -> &Vec<u8> {
    let v = vec![1, 2, 3];
    &v
}
```

Does this compile?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
What happens to `v` when `make` returns?
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**No.** `v` is dropped at the closing brace; returning `&v` would hand the caller a reference to freed memory — a use-after-free in C terms (using memory after it's been freed). The borrow checker rejects this at compile time (`error: missing lifetime specifier` plus `cannot return reference to local variable \`v\``).

If you need to return owned data, return `Vec<u8>` directly, not `&Vec<u8>`.
:::

---

## 13. Mutating while iterating

```rust
let mut v = vec![1u8, 2, 3];
for x in &v {
    v.push(*x);
}
```

Does this compile?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
`for x in &v` holds a shared borrow of `v` for the duration of the loop. `v.push(...)` wants a mutable borrow.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**No.** The `for` loop holds a shared borrow of `v` for as long as it's iterating, so `v.push(...)` inside the loop body needs a mutable borrow at the same time — the same conflict as Q6. (`error[E0502]: cannot borrow \`v\` as mutable because it is also borrowed as immutable`.)

This is the same bug class that causes `ConcurrentModificationException` in Java and silent corruption when modifying a `pandas.DataFrame` mid-iteration. Rust catches it at compile time.
:::

---

## 14. `String` and `&str`

```rust
let s: String = String::from("ACGT");
let r: &str = &s;
println!("{} {}", s, r);
```

Does this compile? What's the relationship between `s` and `r`?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
`&str` is a borrowed view (a *fat pointer* — pointer plus length) into someone else's UTF-8 bytes. `String` owns its bytes on the heap.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**Yes, it compiles, and prints `ACGT ACGT`.** `s` owns the heap allocation holding the bytes `ACGT`. `r` is a shared borrow viewing the same bytes — no copy, no extra allocation. As long as `r` is alive, `s` cannot be mutated or moved.
:::

---

## 15. When is heap memory freed?

```rust
fn main() {
    {
        let v = vec![1u8, 2, 3];
        println!("{:?}", v);
    } // <-- when does v's heap allocation get freed?
    println!("done");
}
```

When does `v`'s heap allocation get freed?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
What happens at the closing brace of a scope?
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**At the inner closing brace `}`** — the moment `v` goes out of scope. The compiler inserted a call to `Vec`'s destructor (Rust calls this *dropping* the value), which frees the heap allocation. No garbage collector, no manual `free()`, no leak. By the time `done` prints, the allocation is already gone.

This is the heart of Rust's memory model: ownership decides who is responsible for freeing, and the compiler inserts the free call at the unique scope where the owner dies.
:::

---

## Where this leads

If most of those felt obvious, you've already absorbed the borrow checker's worldview. If some were surprising, that's fine — the four exercises this afternoon (reverse-complement, buffer reuse, k-mers as slices, quality trim) drill the same rules with biological data until they become reflex.

If you got tripped up by Q11 (`cannot move out of index`) or Q13 (mutate while iterating), expect to see the same compile errors in your own code today. The fix is always one of: borrow, clone, or consume — covered on the [day 2 concepts page](00-concepts.qmd).
